items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.correct: A protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.
items.misconception: A protein with the most calories.
items.random_wrong: Any protein from animal sources only.
items.why: Students think 'complete' means 'high quantity' or 'animal'. It means containing all ESSENTIAL amino acids the body can't make. Most animal proteins are complete; many plants need combining (e.g., rice + beans).
Students confuse fat- and water-soluble vitamins. Mnemonic: ADEK fat-soluble (stored in body fat - can build up to toxic levels). B and C water-soluble (excess excreted in urine).

items.q: Which fat is most linked to heart disease?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Trans fat. “Unsaturated” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: A complete amino acid profile. “No protein” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate UK target percentage of energy from carbohydrates?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
